sentence: Subjective:
The patient, a 57-year-old male, presents with a 2-week history of shortness of breath.
He reports waking up with shortness of breath, which persists upon exertion.
He has been coughing up blood for approximately 3 weeks, with a quantity of 3-4 cups per day.
He denies any fever, bruising, or bleeding from other sites.
He has a history of diabetes, high blood pressure, and asthma.
His blood sugar has been running high, with morning readings around 150 and post-meal readings over 200.
He is taking metformin and norvasc, but has been skipping doses due to low blood pressure.
He has a history of knee problems, but no swelling.
He has not been to the hospital or emergency room for this episode.
Objective:
Vital signs: Pulse 70, oxygen saturation 94%.

Physical exam: No JVD, no carotid bruits, lung exam reveals rails and rhonchi on the right side, diminished breath sounds on the right side, 2/6 systolic ejection murmur, regular rate and rhythm, soft belly, 1+ nonpitting edema on both lower extremities, no calf tenderness, no negative Homan sign.
Assessment / Problem List:
The patient has an acute respiratory infection, likely pneumonia, with coughing up blood, and poorly controlled diabetes, with hemoglobin A1c of 7.7.
The patient's blood pressure is also elevated, with a reading of 120/70.
Plan:
I prescribed a chest X-ray and referred the patient to the emergency department for further evaluation and blood testing.
I also ordered a CAT scan of the chest to rule out a blood clot.
I increased the patient's dose of metformin to 750 mg twice a day and instructed him to hold his norvasc for the time being.
I will have him return in three weeks to discuss his diabetes treatment plan and reassess his blood pressure once the current episode is under control.
Follow-up:
I will have the patient return to the clinic in three weeks to discuss his diabetes treatment plan and reassess his blood pressure.
